draft.introduction: Sappho’s Fragment 1, the Hymn to Aphrodite, is the only complete surviving poem by the greatest lyric poet of antiquity. It invokes the goddess of love as a personal ally, blending religious hymn with intimate desire.

translation.text: Deathless Aphrodite on your richly wrought throne, child of Zeus, weaver of wiles, I beg you, do not with sorrows and torments break my spirit, O Lady. But come here, if ever before you heard my voice from afar and hearkened, and leaving your father’s golden house, came yoked in your chariot. ‘Who wrongs you, Sappho? For if she flees, soon she shall pursue.’
translation.original_text: ποικιλόθρον’ ἀθανάτ’ Ἀφροδίτα παῖ Δίος δολόπλοκε λίσσομαί σε

translator_notes.note: Sappho addresses Aphrodite with the formal structure of a cultic hymn but fills it with personal erotic content. The goddess’s gentle mockery—“Who wrongs you this time, Sappho?”—implies repeated invocations, creating an intimate history between mortal and divine.
